When he died in 1642, the Inquisition at first refused to allow his burial on consecrated ground. They later relented, and his patron Grand Duke Ferdinando II planned a large memorial to him, and money was collected for a tomb.2 However Pope Urban VIII refused to allow it to be built. So Galileo was buried in an unmarked corner of the Medici Chapel in the church of Santa Croce, and it took some 30 years before any inscription was added to mark his burial place.  ...    tomb

title: Manipulating Memories: Postponed Tombs for Galileo and Machiavelli
highlights:
* pp 537–543 * Cite this chapter
Memory & Oblivion * Arjan R. de Koomen2
* 81Accesses
## Abstract Despite their renown, both Niccolo Machiavelli (†l527) and Galileo Galilei (†1642) remained for a long time buried in anonymity in the Florentine church of Santa Croce. Ecclesiastical hostility made the erection of a proper funerary monument inconceivable. Those who dedicated themselves to contesting the disgrace of an absent tomb had to face the dilemma of commemorating a secular hero in the consecrated space of a church. In order to gain ecclesiastical consent,  ...    authorities, but nevertheless testify to the independent and modern thinking of both men.
